Name: adaptive_budgeted_archive_ocr
Title: Adaptive Budgeted OCR for Chinese Handwritten Archives: When Should a Laptop-Scale OCR System Spend More Compute?
Short Hypothesis: A major inefficiency in end-to-end handwritten archive OCR is that current lightweight systems spend roughly uniform compute on easy and hard text regions. We hypothesize that, under strict single-device MPS constraints, a budgeted pipeline that predicts line difficulty and conditionally allocates heavier recognition, stronger decoding, and optional rectification only to uncertain lines will achieve a better accuracy-latency-memory Pareto frontier than uniformly using either a tiny model or a compressed larger model. This is the right setting because archive OCR naturally contains large heterogeneity in difficulty across lines, and conditional compute can be implemented with small public datasets and lightweight modules, without requiring private large-scale pretraining.
Related Work: Prior handwritten Chinese OCR pipelines, including Zhao (2025), focus on improving average detector/recognizer quality and then compressing the final model, e.g., with magnitude pruning. Lightweight OCR work also studies small CNN/Transformer recognizers, while model compression literature examines pruning, quantization, and distillation. However, these lines mostly assume static inference cost per page. Dynamic inference has been explored broadly in computer vision and NLP via early exiting or cascades, but, to our knowledge, has not been systematically studied for compute-aware Chinese handwritten archive OCR with page-level line heterogeneity, public-data-only training, and real wall-clock evaluation on Apple Silicon MPS. The proposal is not a trivial extension of pruning or distillation because the key claim is not merely that models can be made smaller, but that routing uncertainty-aware computation across lines yields superior practical trade-offs and reveals where heavier modules actually matter.
Abstract: Chinese handwritten archive OCR must balance accuracy, robustness, and deployability on modest hardware. Existing end-to-end systems improve detector and recognizer quality and then compress them, but still apply nearly uniform computation to all lines despite large variation in difficulty caused by layout complexity, bleed-through, faded ink, cursive writing, and rare characters. We propose Adaptive Budgeted OCR, a conditional-compute framework for public-data Chinese handwritten archive recognition designed for a single Apple Silicon device. The system uses a lightweight page detector and tiny line recognizer as the default path, then invokes additional computation only for difficult lines identified by uncertainty signals such as CTC entropy, margin, line aspect anomalies, or detector confidence. Hard lines can receive one or more optional upgrades: lightweight geometric rectification, a stronger recognizer, or low-cost language-model rescoring. The central question is simple: can selective computation outperform uniform compression under strict laptop-scale constraints? We will benchmark tiny CRNN/TCN/Conformer recognizers, structured pruning, post-training quantization, degradation-aware synthetic pretraining, and uncertainty-based routing under fixed training-time and inference-time budgets. Evaluation will emphasize character error rate, AR/CR, page latency, memory, and cross-writer/source robustness on public Chinese handwriting datasets and synthetic archive-style pages. The expected outcome is a practical recipe for maximizing OCR quality per unit compute, plus an analysis of which failure modes truly justify extra computation. This would offer a publishable compute-aware perspective beyond static lightweighting and compression.
Experiments: 1. Build a static baseline suite under the same budget: train three small recognizers for line OCR—CRNN+CTC, DenseNet+TCN-tiny, and Conformer-CTC-tiny—using 5k-50k public or synthetic line images, identical preprocessing, max 10 epochs, batch <=16, on MPS. Report CER, AR, CR, params, estimated MACs, training time, latency per line, and peak memory.

2. Train a difficulty predictor for routing. Use cheap uncertainty features from the tiny recognizer and detector: mean/max CTC entropy, top-2 logit margin, normalized blank ratio, detector confidence, predicted line height/width, and simple image statistics. Compare threshold-based routing versus a small MLP predicting whether the stronger recognizer would correct the tiny recognizer. Label a line as hard if the tiny model's CER exceeds a threshold or if the stronger model fixes the output. Metrics: AUROC for hard-line prediction, selective risk-coverage curves, and added routing overhead in ms.

3. Conditional recognizer cascade. At inference, send all lines through the tiny recognizer, then route only the top p% hardest lines to a stronger recognizer (e.g., Conformer-tiny or unpruned DenseNet+TCN-small). Sweep p from 0% to 100% and compare against static tiny-only and strong-only baselines at equal average latency budgets. Main metrics: page-level CER, AR, CR, latency/page, memory, and CER-latency Pareto curves.

4. Optional module ablations for hard lines only. Add two lightweight upgrades individually and jointly: (a) geometric rectification via a tiny TPS/affine head, and (b) character n-gram LM rescoring with beam search plus optional radical-validity constraints from a compact character decomposition lexicon. Evaluate whether these are more cost-effective when applied selectively rather than globally. Metrics: CER improvement per added ms and per added MB.

5. Compression-aware dynamic inference. Apply structured channel pruning to the strong recognizer at several sparsity levels, then evaluate whether a cascade of tiny + pruned-strong beats tiny + unpruned-strong and single pruned models. Add fp16 and, where supported stably, dynamic/int8 post-training quantization on CPU fallback for comparison. Distinguish parameter reduction from real MPS wall-clock gains.

6. Augmentation interaction study. Compare four training regimes for the tiny and strong recognizers: public-only, synthetic-only, public+synthetic, and public+synthetic with physics-inspired degradations (bleed-through, stains, blur, contrast decay, paper texture, stroke perturbation). Test whether better-calibrated uncertainty and better routing emerge when degradations are included. Metrics: CER, expected calibration error, Brier score, and cascade benefit under cross-source evaluation.

7. Generalization under LOWO/LOSO-style splits where metadata allows. Measure whether dynamic routing especially helps unseen writers or sources by computing coverage of routed lines, CER on easy vs hard subsets, and error reduction on rare/degraded samples. If writer/source metadata are limited, simulate domain shift by training on clean synthetic/public subsets and testing on degradation-heavy subsets.

8. Failure-mode analysis. Categorize errors into segmentation failures, severe distortion, cursive fusion, rare-character substitution, and LM-induced errors. For each category, measure whether extra compute helps. This supports a practical claim: not all failures deserve a larger recognizer, and some are detector- or lexicon-limited.

9. Real-device deployment benchmark on Apple Silicon MPS. Implement the full page pipeline with line batching and report end-to-end inference latency per page, throughput at batch sizes 1/4/8, unified memory peak, and robustness of fp16 autocast. Compare static versus adaptive systems under the same average page budget.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Public page-level Chinese handwritten archive datasets are limited; some evaluation may rely on synthetic pages or line-level corpora, reducing direct comparability to Zhao's private archive setting.
Dynamic routing may improve average latency but not worst-case latency; some deployment scenarios may prefer deterministic runtimes.
Uncertainty estimates from CTC models may be poorly calibrated, making routing noisy; calibration losses or temperature scaling may be needed.
Apple MPS support for some quantization paths is limited, so true int8 speedups may require CPU comparison or may not materialize on-device.
The stronger recognizer must be sufficiently complementary to the tiny recognizer; if both make similar errors, the cascade adds little value.
Metadata for rigorous leave-one-writer-out or leave-one-source-out splits may be incomplete in public datasets.
Conditional pipelines are more complex to implement and analyze than single-model baselines, so the paper must clearly demonstrate that complexity yields meaningful practical benefit.

Name: retrieval_augmented_archive_ocr
Title: Retrieval-Augmented Chinese Handwritten Archive OCR: Can Tiny Recognizers Borrow Class Prototypes Instead of Adding Parameters?
Short Hypothesis: A small Chinese handwritten line recognizer fails disproportionately on rare characters, writer-specific glyphs, and degraded strokes because its fixed parameters must memorize too many visual variants under tight compute budgets. We hypothesize that, instead of enlarging the recognizer, a tiny CTC recognizer can query a lightweight external memory of character and radical prototypes built from public isolated-character data and synthetic variants, and use the retrieved prototypes only at uncertain time steps to improve decoding. This direction is needed because the central bottleneck in laptop-scale Chinese archive OCR is not only sequence modeling but visual coverage of a very large character set; prototype retrieval is a more direct and cheaper way to test that hypothesis than simply scaling model size, and it is feasible with public data and single-device MPS training.
Related Work: Zhao (2025) improves handwritten archive OCR through better detection, DenseNet+TCN recognition, and magnitude pruning, but keeps recognition fully parametric. Lightweight OCR work studies CRNNs, CNN-Transformer hybrids, Conformer-CTC models, compression, and synthetic augmentation, while decoder improvements often rely on language models or lexicons. Separate lines of work in few-shot learning and metric learning use class prototypes, and kNN-augmented language models show that external memory can complement small parametric models. However, to our knowledge, Chinese handwritten archive OCR has not been framed as a retrieval problem over public isolated-character prototypes integrated into line-level CTC decoding under strict laptop-scale constraints. This is not a trivial extension of LM rescoring: language models only constrain plausible sequences, whereas the proposed memory directly injects visual evidence for ambiguous characters and rare glyph families without increasing the recognizer depth.
Abstract: Chinese handwritten archive OCR is constrained by noisy backgrounds, large character vocabularies, writer variability, and limited hardware. Existing lightweight systems mainly trade accuracy for efficiency by shrinking or pruning the recognizer, but this forces a small model to internally memorize thousands of character shapes and style variations. We propose a retrieval-augmented OCR framework for public-data Chinese handwriting that asks a simple question: can a tiny line recognizer stay small by borrowing visual evidence from an external prototype memory? The system trains a compact CTC-based recognizer on handwritten lines while separately building a memory bank from public isolated-character images, radical-aware synthetic variants, and optional writer-style clusters. During inference, only low-confidence time steps trigger retrieval from the memory using a lightweight embedding head. Retrieved character or radical prototypes are fused with recognizer logits through a gated adapter or used to rescore beam candidates. This design preserves the speed of a tiny recognizer on easy cases while targeting its main weakness: confusion among visually similar or rare characters. We will compare parametric scaling, language-model rescoring, and prototype retrieval under the same parameter and latency budgets on public Chinese handwriting datasets and synthetic archive-style pages, with evaluation on cross-writer and degraded settings. Expected outcomes are a practical recipe for improving accuracy-per-parameter on laptop-class hardware and a deeper analysis of whether external visual memory is a better use of limited compute than adding model capacity.
Experiments: 1. Train three <=15M-parameter line recognizers under identical MPS constraints: CRNN+CTC, DenseNet+TCN-tiny, and Conformer-CTC-tiny. Use 5k-50k line images from public corpora and synthetic archive-style lines. Report CER, AR, CR, params, MACs/FLOPs, training time, inference latency per line/page, and peak memory.

2. Build prototype memory banks from public isolated-character images (e.g., CASIA-HWDB subsets) and synthetic font-rendered characters with degradation. Compare three memory granularities: character-level prototypes, radical-level prototypes, and clustered writer/style prototypes. Store compact embeddings computed by a small CNN encoder trained with supervised contrastive or metric loss.

3. Integrate retrieval into decoding in two simple ways: (a) logit fusion, where retrieved prototype similarities are converted into additive bias terms over candidate characters at uncertain CTC steps; (b) beam rescoring, where retrieval is used only for top-k ambiguous positions from the beam. Trigger retrieval only when entropy or top-2 margin crosses a threshold. Measure CER gain versus added latency.

4. Compare retrieval against equal-budget alternatives. For the same added parameter or latency budget, compare: larger recognizer width/depth, small character n-gram LM rescoring, retrieval only, and retrieval+LM. This directly tests whether external visual memory is a better investment than more parameters.

5. Rare and confusing character evaluation. Construct challenge subsets containing low-frequency characters, visually similar pairs, and degradation-heavy samples. Measure per-class CER, top-k recall at uncertain positions, and error reduction specifically on rare/ambiguous characters.

6. Cross-writer and cross-source generalization. Where metadata allows, run LOWO/LOSO-style splits; otherwise simulate domain shift using writer/style-separated synthetic variants and real degraded subsets. Test whether retrieval helps more under style shift than on IID splits.

7. Memory size and compression ablation. Vary prototype-bank size from very small to moderate, and test product-quantized or clustered memory representations to keep retrieval cheap. Report accuracy-latency-memory Pareto curves and identify the smallest useful memory.

8. Detection-agnostic page experiment. Plug the best recognizer into a simple page pipeline with either provided line crops or a lightweight detector. Evaluate whether retrieval gains persist at page level when line segmentation noise is present.

9. Failure analysis. Categorize improvements and failures by rare-character substitution, visually similar confusion, background noise, cursive fusion, and segmentation error. Verify that retrieval mainly helps the first two, clarifying when this approach should or should not be used.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Public page-level handwritten archive datasets are limited, so some evaluation may rely on line-level corpora and synthetic page generation.
If the recognizer embeddings are not well aligned with prototype embeddings, retrieval may add noise rather than help; careful metric-learning design is needed.
CTC time steps do not map perfectly to individual characters, especially in cursive or touching-script regions, which may limit prototype usefulness.
Prototype retrieval may improve accuracy on rare characters but not on common errors caused by line detection or severe geometric distortion.
Apple MPS support for approximate nearest-neighbor libraries may be limited; brute-force retrieval over compact memories may be necessary.
The method depends on having at least moderate isolated-character coverage for the target script; very rare historical variants may still be missing.
If retrieval overhead is too high, gains over a slightly larger recognizer may disappear, so latency-aware triggering is essential.

Name: consistency_gated_archive_ocr
Title: Consistency-Gated OCR for Chinese Handwritten Archives: Use Augmentation Agreement as a Cheap Substitute for Bigger Models
Short Hypothesis: Small handwritten Chinese OCR models often fail not because they lack average capacity, but because they are brittle to nuisance factors such as mild skew, contrast changes, bleed-through, and stroke thickness variation. We hypothesize that, under strict single-device MPS constraints, enforcing and exploiting prediction consistency across a few cheap test-time perturbations will improve accuracy-latency trade-offs more effectively than simply enlarging the recognizer or adding a heavier decoder. The key question is simple: if a line’s transcription changes under tiny appearance-preserving perturbations, can that instability both identify likely errors and provide a self-correction signal? This is the right setting because archive OCR errors are highly sensitivity-driven, the intervention is cheap, needs only public or synthetic data, and directly targets robustness under laptop-scale compute budgets without requiring large pretrained models.
Related Work: Zhao (2025) improves archive OCR with stronger detection, DenseNet+TCN recognition, synthetic transfer, and magnitude pruning, but treats inference as single-view prediction and compression as the main efficiency tool. Prior OCR and HTR work has explored data augmentation, test-time augmentation, self-consistency in vision/language, confidence calibration, and lightweight LM rescoring. However, these threads have not been systematically combined for compute-aware Chinese handwritten archive OCR under strict public-data-only, Apple-Silicon-scale constraints. The novelty here is not generic test-time augmentation, but a consistency-gated framework that uses disagreement among a tiny set of archive-relevant perturbations as both (i) a confidence estimator and (ii) a mechanism for selective correction via agreement-weighted decoding or optional rescoring. This differs from the previously proposed adaptive cascade or retrieval ideas by avoiding extra large models or external memory; the core claim is that robustness structure in the input can be mined as a cheap source of error detection and correction.
Abstract: Chinese handwritten archive OCR must operate under severe visual variability while remaining deployable on modest hardware. Existing lightweight systems mainly improve the detector or recognizer and then compress parameters, but a single forward pass remains fragile to small nuisance changes such as contrast shifts, paper aging artifacts, local blur, or slight geometric distortion. We propose Consistency-Gated OCR, a compute-aware framework that asks whether prediction stability under a few cheap archive-style perturbations can substitute for model size. A compact line recognizer is trained with standard augmentation plus an optional consistency regularizer that encourages similar character posterior sequences across paired perturbations. At inference, each line is evaluated under a small set of appearance-preserving transforms, such as slight binarization shift, contrast change, mild blur, or tiny deskew. If predictions agree, the system accepts the base output and spends minimal compute. If they disagree, the line is flagged as unreliable and corrected using agreement-weighted logit fusion, beam rescoring, or optional lightweight language-model assistance. The method requires no heavy secondary recognizer and is well suited to Apple MPS because it trades a small number of tiny forward passes for large increases in parameter count. We will benchmark recognizer families, perturbation sets, consistency objectives, and selective correction policies on public Chinese handwriting corpora and synthetic archive-style pages. The expected outcome is a practical recipe showing when multi-view self-consistency is a better use of scarce compute than scaling or pruning alone, along with an analysis of failure modes where robustness-based correction helps or fails.
Experiments: 1. Train compact line recognizers under identical budget: CRNN+CTC, DenseNet+TCN-tiny, and Conformer-CTC-tiny (all <=15M params when possible) on 5k-50k public or synthetic Chinese line images, max 10 epochs, batch <=16 on MPS. Report CER, AR, CR, params, FLOPs/MACs, MPS training time, inference latency, and peak memory.

2. Consistency-regularized training. For each recognizer, add a paired-view loss: generate two archive-style perturbations of the same line (e.g., mild blur, contrast decay, stroke dilation/erosion, small skew, paper texture overlay) and penalize divergence between framewise CTC posterior distributions or intermediate embeddings, alongside normal CTC loss. Compare baseline augmentation versus augmentation+consistency regularization. Metrics: CER/AR/CR, expected calibration error, and robustness on corruption sweeps.

3. Consistency-gated inference. For each test line, run 2-4 cheap perturbation views plus the original image. Compute disagreement features such as normalized edit distance among greedy decodes, CTC entropy variance, and top-1 vote share. Evaluate how well disagreement predicts recognition error using AUROC, AUPRC, and risk-coverage curves.

4. Cheap self-correction mechanisms. For unstable lines only, compare three correction rules: (a) majority vote over decoded strings, (b) agreement-weighted logit averaging before CTC decoding, and (c) small character n-gram LM rescoring applied only to inconsistent lines. Compare against always-on LM rescoring and against simply widening the recognizer under equal latency budget.

5. Perturbation design ablation. Test which tiny perturbation sets give best benefit-cost ratio: photometric only, geometric only, degradation only, and mixed. Limit each transform to archive-plausible ranges so labels remain unchanged. Measure CER gain per added ms and identify the smallest useful perturbation bundle.

6. Compression interaction study. Apply structured channel pruning and fp16 inference to the best recognizer, then measure whether consistency-gated correction recovers more accuracy from pruning than standard single-view decoding. This directly tests whether robustness-based correction is a better companion to compression than larger backbones.

7. Cross-writer/cross-source robustness. Where metadata allow, evaluate LOWO/LOSO splits; otherwise simulate domain shift by testing on held-out degradation regimes or distinct public subsets. Measure whether consistency gating especially helps under writer/style/source shift.

8. Page-level evaluation with lightweight detection or provided line crops. Integrate the best recognizer into a simple page OCR pipeline and compare single-view versus consistency-gated decoding on page CER/AR/CR and detection-aware end-to-end latency. Include memory and ms/page on MPS.

9. Failure analysis. Group errors into segmentation noise, severe blur, low contrast, touching characters, rare characters, and layout-induced distortions. Determine which categories are detectable by inconsistency and which require other interventions.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Test-time multi-view inference increases latency; gains must be reported on Pareto curves and compared to simply using a slightly larger model.
If perturbations are poorly chosen, they may change the label or inject noise, hurting both calibration and correction.
CTC outputs can be unstable for reasons unrelated to correctness, so disagreement may be an imperfect error detector.
Benefits may be smaller on very clean datasets and larger only on degradation-heavy archive-style inputs.
Apple MPS performance for many small sequential forward passes may be less favorable than batching; implementation must batch perturbation views together.
This method primarily addresses recognizer brittleness, not major detection/layout failures.
Because public archive datasets are limited, some conclusions may rely on synthetic degradations; realism of those degradations is a potential limitation.

Name: error_repair_archive_ocr
Title: Don’t Make the Recognizer Bigger—Teach It to Repair Itself: Error-Driven Post-OCR Correction for Compute-Aware Chinese Handwritten Archive OCR
Short Hypothesis: In laptop-scale Chinese handwritten archive OCR, a large share of residual errors from small CTC recognizers are systematic and locally repairable: visually similar character substitutions, uncertainty-driven deletions/insertions, and short implausible sequences. We hypothesize that a tiny post-OCR repair model, trained on recognizer-generated errors and invoked selectively on uncertain lines, will recover more CER/AR/CR per millisecond and per parameter than spending the same budget on a larger recognizer. This is the right testbed because the visual recognizer is already the main compute bottleneck under Apple MPS constraints, while paired noisy-clean correction data can be generated cheaply from public and synthetic corpora without private archives or large pretraining.
Related Work: Zhao (2025) improves Chinese handwritten archive OCR via stronger detection, DenseNet+TCN recognition, synthetic transfer, and magnitude pruning, but keeps improvement pressure on the visual model itself. OCR literature has long used language models, lexicons, and occasional post-correction, but mostly for printed OCR, larger systems, or generic text cleanup rather than recognizer-specific repair under strict hardware budgets. The key distinction here is to treat post-correction as a first-class compute allocation strategy for handwritten Chinese archives: the corrector is trained on the actual error distribution of compressed recognizers, can use token confidence and top-k alternatives, and is evaluated directly against equal-budget recognizer scaling. This is not a trivial LM-rescoring variant, because the model learns conditional edit behavior tied to OCR confusions rather than only fluency, and it explicitly asks which residual errors are cheaper to fix after recognition than before.
Abstract: Chinese handwritten archive OCR is difficult yet often must run on modest hardware. Existing lightweight pipelines primarily improve or compress the detector and recognizer, implicitly assuming that better OCR mainly requires better visual models. We propose a complementary compute-aware strategy: keep the recognizer small and repair its predictable mistakes with a tiny post-OCR corrector. The core idea is simple: compressed Chinese handwritten recognizers often leave structured residual errors, including visually similar substitutions, dropped characters around low-confidence regions, and short locally implausible strings. These may be easier to fix with sequence-level correction than with a larger visual backbone. We therefore generate paired noisy-clean data automatically by running lightweight recognizers on public and synthetic Chinese handwriting corpora, then train small repair models that consume the OCR string, token confidences, and optional top-k alternatives. We compare confusion-rule baselines, n-gram rescoring, a tiny edit tagger, and a small sequence corrector, and evaluate both always-on and uncertainty-triggered correction under strict Apple MPS limits. The main question is whether post-recognition repair yields a better accuracy-latency-memory trade-off than enlarging or unpruning the recognizer. Experiments on public and synthetic archive-style data will report CER, AR, CR, latency, memory, and cross-writer/source robustness. The expected contribution is a practical recipe for when post-OCR correction is the best use of scarce compute, plus a decomposition of archive OCR errors into those repairable by cheap sequence modeling versus those requiring better vision or layout analysis.
Experiments: 1. Train fixed lightweight recognizer baselines under the MPS budget: tiny CRNN+CTC, DenseNet+TCN-tiny, and Conformer-CTC-tiny on 5k-50k public or synthetic Chinese line images, max 10 epochs, batch <=16. Save greedy outputs, character confidences, and top-k alternatives when feasible. Report CER, AR, CR, params, MACs/FLOPs, training time, latency, and peak memory.

2. Build paired post-correction data by running each recognizer on train/validation lines to obtain noisy OCR outputs aligned with ground truth. Derive confusion statistics and tag common error types: substitution, deletion, insertion, repetition, and punctuation/blank collapse. This data creation is automatic and cheap.

3. Compare lightweight repair methods: (a) character n-gram LM rescoring, (b) confusion-matrix weighted finite-state or rule-based correction, (c) tiny BiTCN or BiGRU edit tagger predicting keep/replace/delete/insert operations, and (d) tiny Transformer corrector <=5M params. Inputs should be incrementally ablated: noisy text only; text + token confidence; text + confidence + top-k alternatives. Main metrics: corrected CER, AR, CR, added latency, params, and memory.

4. Equal-budget comparison: for the same added parameter or latency budget, compare three options—wider/deeper recognizer, LM-only rescoring, and recognizer + learned repair. Plot CER-latency and CER-parameter Pareto curves. This is the central validation of the hypothesis.

5. Selective repair: trigger the corrector only for uncertain lines using mean confidence, CTC entropy, top-2 margin, or blank ratio. Compare always-on versus selective correction and report AUROC for error detection, risk-coverage curves, CER gain per ms, and page-level latency.

6. Train on different noise sources: (a) real recognizer outputs only, (b) synthetic corruptions sampled from observed confusion patterns, and (c) mixed supervision. Test which source best transfers across recognizer families and held-out datasets.

7. Cross-recognizer transfer: train a repair model on one recognizer’s errors and test on another, then on pooled errors. This reveals whether the corrector captures universal Chinese handwritten OCR confusions or model-specific artifacts.

8. Cross-writer/source robustness: where metadata allow, use LOWO/LOSO splits; otherwise use domain-shifted synthetic vs real and degradation-held-out splits. Measure whether post-correction gains persist under writer/style/source shift.

9. Minimal visual side-information ablation: for uncertain spans only, add a tiny CNN embedding from the corresponding image crop and compare against text-only correction. This tests whether a small amount of targeted visual evidence helps more than a fully larger recognizer.

10. Failure analysis: quantify when correction helps or harms, especially on rare names, historical variants, segmentation failures, and severe layout errors. Report category-wise gains and examples of over-correction.
Risk Factors and Limitations: If most residual errors come from bad line detection or severe segmentation failures, sequence repair will have limited impact.
The corrector may over-regularize rare names, archaic forms, or valid low-frequency characters, especially on archive-like text.
Public corpora may not match real archive language distributions, so text-level correction could partly learn dataset priors.
Token-level confidence and top-k alternatives from CTC models can be noisy, limiting the value of uncertainty-aware correction.
Some repair architectures may add implementation complexity without beating simple confusion rules or LM rescoring; strong simple baselines are essential.
Apple MPS support may favor simpler sequence models like BiGRU/TCN over tiny Transformers in wall-clock terms.
This method complements rather than replaces better detection, rectification, or recognizer design.

Name: fidelity_scaling_archive_ocr
Title: Fidelity Scaling for Chinese Handwritten Archive OCR: Match Input Resolution to Stroke Complexity Instead of Scaling the Model
Short Hypothesis: Small OCR models waste compute by processing all handwritten lines at a uniformly high visual fidelity, even though many archive lines are recognizable at much lower resolution while only a minority require fine stroke detail. We hypothesize that a lightweight line recognizer trained with stochastic multi-resolution inputs, plus a tiny policy that predicts the minimum sufficient input fidelity per line, will outperform uniform high-resolution or uniformly compressed baselines in the accuracy-latency-memory trade-off on laptop-class Apple MPS hardware. This setting is necessary because Chinese handwritten OCR is unusually sensitive to stroke-level detail, so input fidelity is a first-class compute variable; unlike simply shrinking the recognizer, fidelity scaling directly tests whether the bottleneck is excess visual resolution rather than insufficient model capacity, and it can be investigated fully with public and synthetic data under strict single-device constraints.
Related Work: Zhao (2025) studies stronger detection, DenseNet+TCN recognition, synthetic transfer, and magnitude pruning for handwritten archive OCR, but keeps input fidelity largely fixed and treats efficiency mainly through model compression. Broader OCR/HTR work explores lightweight recognizers, multi-scale features, image resizing, and test-time augmentation, while dynamic-resolution methods have appeared in generic vision tasks. However, there appears to be little systematic study of resolution as a controllable compute budget specifically for Chinese handwritten archive OCR, where thin strokes, rare character detail, and degradation make the resolution-accuracy trade-off unusually consequential. This proposal is not a trivial image-resizing baseline: the core contribution is a learned minimum-fidelity policy, recognizer training explicitly designed to be robust across fidelities, and real-device Pareto evaluation against equal-budget alternatives such as wider recognizers or pruning.
Abstract: Chinese handwritten archive OCR is difficult not only because of layout noise and writer variability, but also because recognition quality depends strongly on preserving fine stroke detail across a large character vocabulary. Existing lightweight approaches mostly reduce parameters through smaller backbones or pruning, while still processing every line at a fixed visual fidelity. We propose Fidelity Scaling, a compute-aware OCR framework based on a simple question: should every line be read at the same resolution? Our approach trains a compact CTC recognizer on a mixture of line resolutions and degradations so that it can operate reliably across multiple fidelity levels. A tiny policy network then predicts, for each detected line, the lowest input resolution likely to preserve transcription quality based on cheap image features and optional recognizer confidence. Easy lines are recognized at low or medium resolution, while dense, cursive, or degradation-heavy lines are escalated to higher fidelity. This shifts compute control from model size alone to the interaction between visual detail and line difficulty. We will compare fixed-resolution baselines, learned fidelity policies, structured pruning, and lightweight language-model rescoring on public Chinese handwriting datasets and synthetic archive-style pages, all within single-device Apple MPS constraints. Evaluation will emphasize CER, AR/CR, latency, memory, and cross-writer/source robustness. The expected outcome is a practical recipe showing when adjusting input fidelity is a better use of scarce compute than enlarging or merely compressing the recognizer, along with new evidence about which archive OCR failures truly require more visual detail.
Experiments: 1. Train three compact recognizers under identical MPS constraints on 5k-50k public or synthetic Chinese line images: CRNN+CTC, DenseNet+TCN-tiny, and Conformer-CTC-tiny. For each, compare fixed input heights/width caps such as 24, 32, and 48 pixels in normalized line height while holding decoding constant. Report CER, AR, CR, params, estimated MACs, training time, inference latency, and peak memory.

2. Multi-fidelity training. Modify the best one or two recognizers so that each training line is randomly sampled at one of several resolutions and degraded with archive-style perturbations. Compare against single-resolution training to test whether explicit fidelity robustness prevents large drops at low resolution. Metrics: CER across each resolution bucket, calibration, and robustness under held-out degradations.

3. Minimum-sufficient-fidelity analysis. For each validation line, run the recognizer at several resolutions and record the lowest resolution whose prediction matches the highest-resolution prediction or ground truth within a small CER tolerance. Analyze correlations between required fidelity and simple line properties: stroke density, connected-component count, foreground ratio, estimated blur, line length, and entropy. This establishes whether fidelity is predictable from cheap features.

4. Learned fidelity policy. Train a tiny MLP or MobileNet-style policy network to choose low/medium/high resolution per line from cheap grayscale thumbnails and handcrafted image statistics. Compare supervised targets from Experiment 3 against reinforcement-free cost-sensitive classification that penalizes unnecessary high resolution. Evaluate policy accuracy, achieved average resolution, page latency, and final CER/AR/CR.

5. Confidence-based escalation baseline. Implement a simple two-pass system: first recognize every line at low resolution, then rerun only uncertain lines at medium or high resolution based on CTC entropy, margin, or blank ratio. Compare this heuristic to the learned one-shot policy and to always-high-resolution recognition. This tests whether the gains come from fidelity control itself or from the policy sophistication.

6. Equal-budget comparison against model scaling and compression. For the same latency or memory budget, compare: (a) higher-fidelity tiny recognizer, (b) lower-fidelity slightly larger recognizer, (c) structured-pruned recognizer at high fidelity, and (d) adaptive-fidelity recognizer. Plot CER-latency and CER-memory Pareto curves on MPS. This directly answers whether fidelity scaling is a better knob than parameter scaling.

7. Interaction with synthetic generation and degradation augmentation. Train with four data regimes: public only, synthetic only, public+synthetic, and public+synthetic with physics-inspired degradations. Test whether degradation-aware training changes the optimal fidelity policy, especially for faded or bleed-through-heavy lines.

8. Cross-writer and cross-source robustness. Where metadata allow, evaluate leave-one-writer-out or leave-one-source-out splits; otherwise simulate shift using held-out degradation styles and different synthetic font families. Measure whether adaptive fidelity helps more on unseen writers/sources than fixed resolution.

9. Optional page-level experiment. Combine a lightweight detector with line-level adaptive fidelity and report detection F1, end-to-end page CER/AR/CR, ms/page, and unified-memory usage. If page datasets are too limited, construct simple synthetic pages with mixed line spacing and noise to validate end-to-end feasibility.

10. Failure analysis. Categorize errors where low fidelity hurts most: visually similar characters, thin strokes, touching characters, rare characters, and degraded ink/background contrast. Quantify how often higher fidelity fixes these versus cases where errors persist and model capacity, not resolution, is the bottleneck.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Public handwritten archive page datasets are limited, so some findings may rely on line-level corpora or synthetic pages rather than real historical page benchmarks.
If most lines already require high resolution, adaptive fidelity may provide little speedup; the paper must show the distribution of minimum sufficient fidelity to justify the premise.
Very low resolutions may alter sequence length and CTC alignment properties, making comparisons sensitive to preprocessing choices.
A learned policy may offer only modest gains over simple confidence-based escalation; strong heuristic baselines are therefore essential.
Improving latency through lower resolution may be smaller on MPS than expected if preprocessing or data movement dominates runtime.
The approach mainly targets recognizer compute and may not address major page-layout or detection failures.
Synthetic degradations and font-rendered lines may not fully capture rare historical glyph variation, limiting external validity of some conclusions.
